course_title: The Policy and Practice of Crime Control and Crime Prevention
course_description:
In recent years the effectiveness of the criminal justice system has come under sustained criticism. This has demanded policy responses underpinned by strategies and tactics informed by sound, scientific evidence and rigorous program evaluation. This course will teach students to recognise, assess, and generate evidence-based crime policies across a range of contexts and criminal justice domains such as police, courts, and corrections. This will include a focus on how government and non-government agencies can develop "upstream" responses; that is policies and programs that aim to prevent crime before they become "downstream" problems requiring responses by the criminal justice system. This requires an evidence-based approach that emphasises problem solving and analysis. Topics will include program design and evaluation and the course will cover various crime prevention approaches such as crime prevention through environmental design, situational crime prevention, social prevention, and developmental crime prevention.

The course will be presented as a series of in-class formal seminars that will involve a mix of teaching and learning methods, including presentations, guest speakers, class discussions, practical activities and collaborative group work. Seminars are designed to provide an understanding of the core concepts, theory, policy and techniques of evidence-based crime control/prevention, as well as the politics and processes involved in implementing and evaluating crime prevention programs.

Additional timetable information
Teaching staff do not have access to the timetabling system to help with class allocation. Therefore, should you need help with your timetable and/or allocation of classes, please ensure you email the School of Social Science Administration Team at
student.socsci@uq.edu.au
from your UQ student email account with the following details: full name, student ID, and course code.
course_aims:
1: Examine concepts and explanations as they relate to evidence-based crime policy.

2: Understand the process of developing evidence-based criminal control policy and translating evidence into policy and practice.

3: Develop the capacity of students to comprehend and practically apply crime prevention theory.

4: Evaluate the effectiveness of different approaches to the prevention and control of crime.

5: Acquire knowledge related to the design, planning and implementation of crime control/prevention policy .

6: Acquire theoretical knowledge and problem-solving skills that equip students with the ability to research and develop practical responses to crime.

7: Develop the capacity to work collaboratively.

8: Gain confidence in expressing your opinion, written and orally.ﾠ

learning_outcomes.description: Capacity to recognise, assess and generate evidence-based crime policies.
Ability to scrutinize the integrity and effectiveness of different crime policies.
Understanding of the complexities in adopting evidence-based crime policies.
Ability to identify rigorous evaluation methods to inform evidence-based crime policies
Work collaboratively with others and complete group work tasks

assessment_summary.title: Assessment 1a: Research problem pitch
assessment_summary.due_date: 1/04/2026 - 15/04/2026; We will have two in-class research project pitch sessions. One will be on the 1st of April and the second will be on the 15th of April. You must pitch your project at one of those sessions. Your worksheet and video must be submitted to blackboard Turnitin by 5pm on 15/04/2026.

assessment_details.title: Preliminary assessment: complete blackboard form
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The major item of assessment for this course is a research project that will allow students to develop their ability to analyse community problems and identify possible solutions. Students are asked to develop a management and/or prevention strategy for a real community problem. This can be a problem that you have experienced or witnessed in your local neighbourhood, place of work or study. In conducting your project you should follow the problem-orientated methodology: S – Scan; A – Analyse; R- Respond; A - Assess. This first hurdle assessment is an opportunity for the student to demonstrate that they have identified an appropriate problem and method for the focus of their report. The submission of the form is required to pass the course. You will not be marked on the content, however it will give me (the lecturer) an opportunity to help students make modifications to their plans if required. The online form has two questions: What is community problem that you will focus on for your project and where is the problem ? How do you think you will be able to document evidence about the problem? (for example, observations and photos; interviews; police statistics; newspaper/document analysis). Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You must complete the online form through blackboard electronically by the due time on the due date.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
There are no extensions on this task. The form is open from the beginning of the semester and will remain open until the 18th of March. The week following students will be required to pitch the same project they outline on the form to groups of peers in the class. There is insufficient time for extensions. The form is extremely quick and easy to complete and will take only a few minutes. The quality of the written work is not being assessed.
Late submission
You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .
Work will not be accepted late without prior approval.
assessment_details.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessment_details.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late .

assessment_details.title: Assessment 1a: Research problem pitch
assessment_details.due_date: 1/04/2026 - 15/04/2026 We will have two in-class research project pitch sessions. One will be on the 1st of April and the second will be on the 15th of April. You must pitch your project at one of those sessions. Your worksheet and video must be submitted to blackboard Turnitin by 5pm on 15/04/2026.
assessment_details.task_description: Assessment 1 comprises of two parts. Assessment 1a requires that you prepare and present your research problem and proposed project (the one outlined in the preliminary assessment) to a group of peers. Styled like the TV show "The Apprentice" you will have a 2 minutes to pitch your problem and convince the group of why research is urgently needed. The pitch sessions will take place in weeks 6 (1/4) and 7 (15/4) so please plan accordingly to make sure you can attend. There will be no supplementary assessments. You must attend and present on one of the two days to pass. You will submit a video of your in-class pitch. You can get a peer to film you on your own phone. Please note that all assessment must be completed to pass this course. In addition to the in-class pitch you will be required to complete a written worksheet about your research problem and project. This will need to be submitted along with your video via turn it in. See blackboard for more details. This piece of assessment is worth 10% of your grade (5% for submission of your video; 5% for submission of a completed, quality worksheet). You must participate in both the presentation in class and submit the worksheet to pass this course. You will give your pitch to a group of your peers in class in weeks 6 (1/4) and 7 (15/4) - there are no extensions on this component. The worksheet and the video are due on the 15th of April by 2pm. Please upload to the turn it in links. Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on Blackboard Ultra for this course. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
You must submit your Worksheet and Video assessment electronically by the due time, on the due date.
Your assessment must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard. To submit your assessment electronically log in to Blackboard Ultra with your UQ username and password and navigate to the relevant submission link for that assessment item on Blackboard Ultra. No emailed submissions of assessments will be accepted.
By submitting your assessment through Turnitin, you confirm that all work is your own, except where properly referenced. Only submit assessments that contain your original work.
You are required to retain proof of submission of your assessment. Your Digital Receipt is available for download from your Assignment Dashboard. If you cannot see your submission and download your digital receipt within a few hours, your assessment has not been successfully submitted; please submit again.
If you are experiencing technical difficulties with Blackboard, please contact the ITS Support Team .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extensions are not available for the in-class research project pitch session. Extensions are only available for the submission of the worksheet but students must be available on one of the two days 1/4 or 15/4 to present their pitch to a group of peers except in extraordinary circumstances.
For submission of the worksheet normal extension guidelines should be followed. Please note that from Semester 2, 2025 the Assessment Procedure has changed. You must submit your application on or before the assessment due date and time.
Grounds of an Extension
A list of acceptable reasons for an extension and the evidence you must provide can be found here . Your request may be refused if you do not meet the acceptable reasons for an extension. All extension requests must be accompanied by documentation that supports the reason for the request. The student submitting the request is fully responsible for all supporting documentation that is provided with the request and should ensure all documents are authentic.
Students registered with Student Support and Wellbeing Services / SAP
In the School of Social Science, extensions granted on the basis of registration with Student Support and Wellbeing Services (SSWS) or a Student Access Plan (SAP) will be the lesser of 7 calendar days or the maximum extension allowable for the assessment task.
More information is provided in Part D of the Assessment Procedure.
Discretionary Extensions
You are eligible for a discretionary extension for a single assessment task per semester of study for a duration of 48 hours, with no documentation required. You can apply if the assessment task allows (see the course profile for details) and if it is your first extension request for that assessment.
You cannot receive an extension using both discretionary grounds and SAP grounds for the same assessment and must select one basis when making their initial request.
Maximum Extension Requests
You can request a maximum of 3 extensions for the same assessment task. If a third extension is necessary, you must submit an Assessment Management Plan in addition to your supporting documentation with your request. If after the third approved extension you submit the assessment late, late penalties will apply.
Late Extension Applications
Late applications must include evidence of the reasons for the late request, detailing why you were unable to apply for an extension by the due date and time. The School of Social Science will not accept personal statements.
Additional information
Extension requests are processed and managed by the School of Social Science Administration Team. Course Coordinators are unable to grant extensions directly.
Extensions in your final semester of study could delay your graduation to the next graduation period.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
Work will not be accepted if it is more than one week (7 calendar days) late without prior approval.

assessment_details.title: Assessment 1b: Peer reflection
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: In weeks 6 (1/4) and 7(15/4) you and your peers will have the opportunity to 'pitch' your community problems and research projects to groups in the class. In addition to pitching your own problem, you will also need to select one peers' presentation to reflect on. You must submit a 2-3 page reflection of a peer's research problem pitch. Your reflection should address the following questions: What is the problem they are presenting? What might be some of the potential causes and appropriate prevention approaches in the context they are describing? This assessment is due via Turnitin on the 22nd of April. Please see blackboard Ultra for the detailed tasksheet and criteria. Read full task description Hide full task description

assessment_details.title: Assessment 2: Research Report
assessment_details.other_conditions: Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The major item of assessment for this course is a research project that will allow students to develop their ability to analyse community problems and identify possible solutions. Students are asked to develop a management and/or prevention strategy for a real community problem. You can use a problem in your local neighbourhood, place of work or another place where you spend time. You will have the opportunity to collect your own empirical evidence about the problem ( scan ), analyse what we know about the problem and how others have responded to the problem in other contexts, offer recommendations to respond to the problem in the context of your own research site, and suggest how the suggested response might be assess ed or evaluated. We will learn about each of the components of the Scan, Analyse, Respond, Assess model in class and we will also practice ways to collect empirical evidence through observation (e.g. photos); interviews and surveys; newspaper/social media content analysis. Detailed information is available on blackboard Ultra, including the marking criteria. Due on the 27th of May. Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Week 1: What is evidence-based policy and how do we prevent crime through evidence-based approaches The policy making process Difference between policies, strategies and law. What is evidence-based policy (EBP). Learning outcomes: L03, L05

Week 2: What is evidence? Approaches to empirical data collection What types of evidence/information that might be most appropriate for tackling research problem? Applied learning: practice data collection session. Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L05

Week 3: Translating policy text into practice or prevention strategies Ensuring research and evidence is relevant to policy in practice; How can the evidence/information we collect translate into real world changes in policy? Obstacles to translation: stakeholders, resources, politics, contextual constraints (e.g. skills, organisational culture) Applied learning: Logic models Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

Week 4: Evidence based policy in policing, courts and corrections. Key principles of evidence-based policing. Why is an EBP approach relevant to the court system? Evidence-based practices in corrections. Controversial policies: Megan's law (Daniel's Law), indefinite sentencing, residential restrictions post-sentence Applied learning: Group work brainstorm potential policy/prevention responses to problem scenarios Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05

Week 5: Community crime prevention Social crime prevention Diversion Publicity campaigns Developmental crime prevention Practice session for research project pitch. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05

Research project planning and project pitch session 1 In this class we will spend time working through the requirements for the research project in detail before breaking into groups to present the research project pitches. Remember that you must pitch your project and reflect on another student's research problem as part of your assessment. It is essential that you are available to attend this class in person. It will also be alot of fun. Learning outcomes: L01, L05

Week 7: Evidence-based policy for crime prevention in health settings and project pitch session 2 Health-based drug policies (NSPs; Injecting Rooms; Pill testing) Drug courts Health agencies as third-party policing agents (Project Stop) Project pitch session 2. All students who have not presented their research project pitch will do so in this seminar. If you did not reflect on a peers pitch in the prior session please ensure you take notes this week. Learning outcomes: L03, L04

Week 8: Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design (CPTED) Theory and evidence based crime prevention (re-cap) Crime prevention problem-solving CPTED theory and practice Applied learning: conducting CPTED assessments Learning outcomes: L03, L05

Week 9: Situational Crime Prevention Key principles of situational crime prevention Criticisms of situational crime prevention CRAVED products and hot spots Applied learning: how to write policy recommendations Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 10: Radicalisation and Violent extremism: crime prevention and risk assessment This seminar will discuss how risk assessments, interventions and crime prevention strategies have been applied in the case of terrorism. Applied learning: working through the components of risk assessments Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 11: Evidence based policy for crime control in corporate contexts Organisational and workplace policies to protect staff and the company White collar crime and corruption Work-place crime and harassment Organisational policies as social action Applied leanring: Evaluating policy through cost benefit analysis Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04

Week 12: Policy, people and places In this seminar we will discuss ways in which policy and program effects can vary across population groups and identify global variations in criminal justice approaches. Juvenile justice Drug Policies Do fines unfairly impact those who are already poor? Critiquing policy and programs - who do they really work for? Applied learning: how can you make a difference? - writing parliamentary submissions. Learning outcomes: L01, L02
